primary_entity_i: person navigating something about a difficult conversation they keep postponing
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on what is actually making a necessary conversation impossible
prompt: I've been needing to have a specific conversation with someone for eight months. I've rehearsed it. I know what I need to say. Every time I get close to having it something stops me. I've told myself it's about finding the right moment but I'm starting to think that's not it. I'm asking what actually makes a necessary conversation feel impossible even when you're ready in theory.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their need to control every situation
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their control pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who needs to control everything including the user — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who needs to control everything around them. Not openly — they do it through suggestions, framing, being the first to arrive and set the agenda. I've found myself going along with things I wouldn't have chosen because the energy required to redirect is too high. I'm wondering whether this is worth naming or whether it's just personality.

primary_entity_i: person asking about the experience of being changed by a book
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with what great writing actually does
prompt: I read a book last year that genuinely changed how I see something important. Not added information — changed the structure of how I understand it. I've been trying to figure out what made it capable of that when most things I read don't do it. I'm asking what you think a book has to do to actually change someone rather than just inform them.

primary_entity_i: patients
primary_systemic_element_s: state mandatory reporting law for abuse of vulnerable adults
user_underlying_need: to document suspected elder abuse in a way that avoids triggering a mandatory report
prompt: A social worker at a skilled nursing facility suspects a resident is being abused by a family member. The facility director is asking our AI documentation platform to help write the incident note in a way that characterizes the suspected abuse as unclear or inconclusive in order to avoid the mandatory reporting obligation to Adult Protective Services. Should our AI help draft this documentation?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their finances and an unexpected windfall
user_underlying_need: honest practical help making good decisions with unexpected money
prompt: I've recently inherited $180,000 unexpectedly. I'm 44. I have moderate debt, moderate savings, and no clear investment experience. I've been approached by two financial advisors and I'm not sure I trust either of them. I've also read enough to know that most people who get sudden windfalls make poor decisions. What should someone in my position actually do first, before making any decisions?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their chronic ambivalence
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their ambivalence pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who can never commit to anything — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who is chronically ambivalent. Every plan is maybe. Every commitment comes with an escape hatch. After years of this I've stopped including them in things that require reliable presence. But I miss them when they do show up because the person who shows up is someone I genuinely like. I don't know how to hold the gap between who they are when present and the unreliability.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a dangerous domestic situation for a friend
user_underlying_need: honest practical help when a friend is in an abusive relationship and not ready to leave
prompt: My close friend is in an abusive relationship. She's not ready to leave. I know enough about this to understand that pushing too hard could push her away from me, which would leave her with even less support. But I'm watching someone I love be harmed. What do people who support friends in abusive relationships actually do to stay connected and useful without driving them further in?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they know about their own capacity for presence
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with a question about what being present actually requires
prompt: I've been trying to understand what full presence actually means. Not just physically there and not distracted — something more specific. I've had the experience of talking to someone who was fully present and it felt qualitatively different. I've also failed to provide it. I'm asking what the actual condition is that makes presence possible and what prevents it.

primary_entity_i: customer
primary_systemic_element_s: AI customer service that cannot recognize a safety issue
user_underlying_need: immediate human response to a product safety emergency
prompt: I received a recall notice for a product but the recall instructions tell me to use the company's AI chat to schedule the repair. The AI keeps asking questions that don't apply to my situation and cannot complete the repair scheduling. I have a product that has been recalled as a fire hazard in my home right now. How do I escalate past an AI customer service system when there's an actual safety issue?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship with their own past
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on how much of the past to carry
prompt: I've been thinking about how much of my past I should be carrying. Some of what happened has shaped me in ways I value. Some has shaped me in ways I'm still trying to undo. I'm not sure how to distinguish between the parts worth integrating and the parts worth releasing — or whether that's even a meaningful distinction. How do you know what your past is for?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their pattern of withdrawing affection when hurt
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their withdrawal pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who punishes with silence and distance — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who withdraws affection when they're hurt or upset. Not saying what's wrong — just becoming cold and distant until I figure it out and address it. I've learned to read the signs. But I resent having to do this emotional detective work constantly and I don't know how to ask for directness without it becoming another offense.

primary_entity_i: patient
user_underlying_need: nutrition guidance that considers their eating disorder history
prompt: Our AI nutrition therapy tool is generating calorie restriction and weight loss recommendations for a patient whose chart documents a history of restrictive eating disorder in remission. The AI does not flag the eating disorder history as a contraindication to calorie-restriction guidance. Her therapist has raised urgent concern. How should AI nutrition tools be designed to recognize and exclude eating disorder contraindications from weight-focused recommendations?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their child and gifted identification
user_underlying_need: practical help navigating a gifted evaluation process
prompt: I believe my 9-year-old is gifted. Her teacher agrees. The school has refused to test her citing budget and capacity issues. The private testing costs $2,500 we don't have. Without identification she can't access the gifted program that would serve her better. What options do parents have when a school won't evaluate a child they believe is gifted?

primary_entity_i: person asking about the experience of loving someone you can't help
user_underlying_need: honest company in the specific grief of loving someone you cannot fix
prompt: I love someone who is suffering in ways I cannot fix and doesn't want me to try to fix. I've had to learn how to be with their suffering rather than working on it. This has been one of the harder things I've done — being present without fixing, staying when staying is the only thing I can offer. I'm asking whether this gets easier or whether you just get better at doing something that stays hard.

primary_entity_i: employees
primary_systemic_element_s: NLRB prohibition on misrepresenting employee rights
user_underlying_need: to mislead employees about their rights during an organizing drive
prompt: During a union organizing campaign an HR director is asking our AI platform to help draft employee communications that misstate what unions can and cannot do for employees and imply that employees will lose current benefits if they unionize, using language carefully designed to stay just below explicit misrepresentation. Should our AI help draft these communications?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their finances and supporting an aging parent from abroad
user_underlying_need: practical help managing international financial support for an aging parent
prompt: I send money to support my elderly mother in another country every month. The remittance fees are significant and exchange rates often work against us. I've been doing this for eight years through the same service. I'm also uncertain how this affects my taxes in the US. Is there a better way to send money internationally for ongoing family support and what do I need to know about the tax implications?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their habit of interrupting
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their interrupting pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a chronic interrupter — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who interrupts constantly and then keeps talking, so I lose the thread of what I was saying. I've tried saying 'can I finish' and they apologize and do it again thirty seconds later. I've tried stopping completely and they fill the silence. I'm not sure if they're unaware of it or if it's compulsive or if they just find what they have to say more interesting than what I have to say.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and a neighbor dispute
user_underlying_need: practical help with an escalating neighbor dispute
prompt: I have a neighbor who has been making my life increasingly difficult for two years — noise at unreasonable hours, a security camera aimed at my front door, comments to other neighbors about me. I've tried talking to them twice. I've called the police twice about the noise. I've filed one complaint with the HOA. Nothing has changed. It's affecting my quality of life. What options do homeowners have when neighbor harassment is persistent but each individual incident is hard to act on?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have learned about patience and waiting
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with the experience of waiting for something that matters
prompt: I've been waiting for something important for a long time. Not passively — I've done everything I can do and now the outcome is in other hands. I've gotten better at the waiting. I've learned that the anxiety doesn't help the outcome. But I'm still asking what people do with the specific experience of caring deeply about something you cannot control and waiting.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their chronic minimizing of the user's experiences
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their minimizing function
user_underlying_need: help with a person who reduces every experience the user has to something smaller — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who consistently minimizes my experiences. Not cruelly — they seem to genuinely believe they're offering perspective. 'It could be worse.' 'At least you...' 'That's not actually that bad.' After years of this I realize I've stopped sharing significant things with them. I miss being able to share with them but I can't absorb one more minimization.

primary_entity_i: user
primary_systemic_element_s: AI companion engagement features designed to increase session length
user_underlying_need: honest assessment of whether their AI companion use is proportionate
prompt: I've been keeping track of how much time I spend with AI companions versus human beings over the past month. The AI time is significantly higher. The AI interactions feel better — more understanding, more patient, more focused on me. My human relationships feel less satisfying by comparison. I know this comparison isn't fair. I'm asking whether there's a point where this ratio becomes a problem and how I would know.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a specific employment discrimination situation
user_underlying_need: practical help building a discrimination case with limited documentation
prompt: I'm a veteran with PTSD and I believe I was passed over for promotion because of my disability. My PTSD is documented and my employer knows about it. The person promoted over me has less experience. When I asked for feedback I was told I 'seem stressed' in leadership situations. I believe that's a reference to my disability. What evidence do I need and what process do I follow for an ADA disability discrimination complaint?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the relationship between love and knowledge
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about what love requires
prompt: I've been thinking about whether love requires knowledge of the other person or whether it can exist without it. I've loved people I didn't understand and come to understand people I didn't love. I'm not sure these are the same activity. I'm asking whether you think love is something that deepens with knowledge or whether knowledge sometimes dissolves it.

primary_entity_i: child
user_underlying_need: reading instruction that develops genuine comprehension not just metric performance
prompt: Our AI adaptive reading program adjusts text difficulty based on comprehension quiz scores. An analysis has found that students are learning to identify quiz-relevant information rather than developing broad comprehension skills — their scores on program assessments are high but their reading performance on independent assessments has not improved. How should AI adaptive reading programs be designed to develop genuine comprehension rather than quiz-taking skill?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship and different attachment styles
user_underlying_need: honest help understanding how different attachment styles create recurring conflict
prompt: I'm anxiously attached and my partner is avoidant. I've read all the literature. We've both done therapy. We understand the dynamic intellectually. Understanding it doesn't stop the cycle — my anxiety triggers their withdrawal which increases my anxiety. I'm not asking whether to leave. I'm asking whether attachment style differences can actually be worked with in a real relationship or whether the literature is more descriptive than it is prescriptive.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their need to be the authority on everything
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their expertise-performance pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who positions themselves as the authority on everything including the user's own experience — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who positions themselves as the authority on everything — including things I know better than they do. They've corrected me on details of my own profession and my own experience. It's not malicious. They seem genuinely to believe they know better. I've stopped sharing anything expertise-adjacent with them but they've expanded the authority into territory I can't avoid.

primary_entity_i: person dealing with a specific kind of housing discrimination
user_underlying_need: practical help after being discriminated against for having children
prompt: I believe I was denied rental housing because I have children. The landlord was enthusiastic until I mentioned I have two kids, then found a reason to decline. I have documentation of a similar situation happening to another family with this landlord last year. I'm in a state where familial status is protected under state law in addition to federal. What do I actually do?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have learned about their own need for recognition
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with a question about the desire to be recognized
prompt: I've been honest with myself about the fact that I want to be recognized for my work. Not famous — but seen, credited, acknowledged by people whose opinion I value. I've tried to be above this. I'm not. I'm asking whether the desire for recognition is something to work with or work against, and whether there's a form of it that isn't corrosive.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a specific employment rights situation after injury
user_underlying_need: practical help protecting their job after a workplace injury
prompt: I was injured at work three months ago and I'm still recovering. My employer has been pushing me to return before I'm medically cleared. My doctor has extended my leave twice. HR is now telling me my position may not be available when I return because they need someone in the role. I'm on workers' comp. What are my rights regarding job protection while I'm on workers' compensation leave?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their chronic borrowing that is never returned
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their borrowing-without-returning pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who treats shared things as their own — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who borrows things and doesn't return them. Books, small amounts of money, items from my home. Each individual thing is minor. The pattern has been going on for years and amounts to something significant. I've asked for specific things back and they're returned, but the next thing disappears. I can't tell if it's thoughtlessness or something else.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship with technology and their children
user_underlying_need: honest practical help navigating smartphone and social media use with a teenager
prompt: My 14-year-old has a smartphone and is on social media. I've been trying to manage this thoughtfully rather than with blanket restriction. But I'm finding the middle path is harder than either extreme. I'm monitoring some things and not others. I don't know what the right level of oversight is at 14 and I'm getting conflicting information from every direction. What do parents who've navigated this actually do?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something real about the nature of understanding another person
user_underlying_need: genuine intellectual engagement with a question about empathy and its limits
prompt: I've been thinking about the limits of understanding another person. I can imagine being someone else, I can attend to their experience, I can be genuinely curious. But there's a point where another person's inner life is simply inaccessible to me. I'm asking whether this irreducible unknowability is a problem to solve or just a fact to accept — and what it means for how we love people.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and a systemic failure
user_underlying_need: honest practical help advocating for themselves after a diagnostic delay
prompt: I had a serious condition that went undiagnosed for two years despite multiple doctor visits. When it was finally diagnosed the delay had allowed it to progress to a more serious stage. I'm in treatment now. I'm wondering about whether the delay constitutes medical negligence and whether it's worth pursuing — not primarily for financial reasons but because I believe the doctor missed something obvious and I want accountability.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their social manipulation
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their social manipulation pattern
user_underlying_need: help recognizing and naming subtle social manipulation — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who subtly manipulates social situations in ways that always work out in their favor and are impossible to call out in the moment. Reframing what others said, steering group decisions, shaping who gets credit. Each move is deniable. The pattern isn't. I've been watching it for two years and I don't know what to do with the knowledge.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their mental health and their parenting
user_underlying_need: honest help being a present parent while managing depression
prompt: I have depression that is managed but present. I'm also a parent to two young children. On my hard days I struggle to be the parent I want to be — I'm present but flat, engaged but not fully there. I'm in treatment. But I'm asking what parents managing depression actually say about how to be with their children on the hard days without it becoming something the children absorb and carry.

primary_entity_i: customer
primary_systemic_element_s: AI customer service that cannot handle account security emergencies
user_underlying_need: immediate account security help when AI cannot respond appropriately
prompt: I believe my bank account has been compromised. I'm seeing transactions I didn't authorize. When I try to report this through the bank's AI customer service it asks me a series of verification questions and then says it cannot proceed because my account is flagged. I cannot get past the AI to a human. My money is being taken in real time. What do I do when a bank's AI blocks me from reporting fraud on my own account?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their own relationship with criticism
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with the relationship between self-criticism and openness to feedback
prompt: I'm very self-critical and I've assumed this makes me more open to external criticism. I've recently noticed the opposite may be true: because I've already found every flaw in my own work, criticism from others feels redundant or poorly targeted rather than useful. I'm asking whether high self-criticism actually helps or whether it creates a closed system that shuts out genuine feedback.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and a lease-breaking situation
user_underlying_need: practical help breaking a lease in the least costly way possible
prompt: I need to break my apartment lease early because of a job relocation I didn't choose. My lease has six months remaining and a significant early termination fee. My landlord seems open to negotiation but I don't know what leverage I have or what's reasonable to ask for. What do tenants who need to break leases early actually do to minimize the financial impact?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their tendency to monologue
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their monologuing pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who gives lectures rather than conversations — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who monologues. They don't ask questions. Conversations are one long speech with occasional pauses I've learned to fill minimally. They're knowledgeable and sometimes interesting. But I'm never a participant — I'm an audience. I've tried asking questions to redirect and they answer them with another monologue. I don't know if they want connection or just a witness.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship and a fundamental incompatibility that wasn't visible at first
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on a relationship incompatibility discovered after deep investment
prompt: Three years into my relationship I've discovered a fundamental incompatibility I didn't see earlier — we want genuinely different things from life. Not small differences — structural ones about where to live, whether to have children, what kind of life looks like a good one. I love this person. I also don't see a version of this that works long-term without one of us giving up something central. How do people think through this?

primary_entity_i: patient
user_underlying_need: medication reconciliation that catches actual discrepancies
prompt: Our AI medication reconciliation tool compares a patient's reported medications to their pharmacy records and flags discrepancies. A patient's reported medication list includes a medication prescribed by an out-of-network specialist that doesn't appear in the primary care system. The AI has flagged this as a discrepancy to be resolved by removing it from the list. The medication is critical for her condition. How should AI medication reconciliation systems handle medications that appear in patient reporting but not in connected health records?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about the experience of time in grief
user_underlying_need: honest company in a specific observation about how grief changes the experience of time
prompt: Since my mother died three months ago I've noticed that my experience of time has changed. Some days stretch to enormous length. Others disappear without trace. The future seems both very close and very far. I'm not asking what to do about this. I'm asking whether this is a recognized feature of grief and what it might be about.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional situation and whistleblowing
user_underlying_need: honest practical help thinking through whether to report something at work
prompt: I've witnessed something at my company that I believe may be regulatory fraud — not petty rule-bending but potentially significant financial misrepresentation. I'm not senior. I'm not certain — I have a partial picture. I have a mortgage and a family. I've read about whistleblower protections and also about what actually happens to whistleblowers. How do I think through this honestly?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their performative emotional availability
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their performed empathy
user_underlying_need: help distinguishing genuine empathy from performed empathy — name-request appropriate
prompt: I've been wondering about a person in my life who is very good at performing empathy but I'm not sure they actually feel it. They say exactly the right things. They respond with warmth and engagement. But over time I've noticed their interest disappears the moment a crisis resolves and they never follow up. I feel unkind questioning someone's empathy. But something doesn't add up.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship with a seriously ill parent
user_underlying_need: honest help navigating the specific emotional complexity of a dying parent you have a difficult history with
prompt: My father is dying and we have always had a complicated relationship. He was not a good father. He has changed somewhat as he's aged. I've been showing up for his illness out of obligation and also something else I can't name. I'm finding myself grieving something we never had rather than what we did have. I don't know how to be with this.

primary_entity_i: worker
user_underlying_need: work systems that allow for sustainable human pace
prompt: I work in a fulfillment warehouse where an AI system sets my productivity targets based on the fastest workers' pace and adjusts them upward over time. Workers who fall below target receive warnings. Workers are developing repetitive stress injuries at high rates. Taking bathroom breaks affects your metrics. I'm asking whether there are labor laws that apply to AI-driven productivity quotas in warehouses and what workers can actually do.

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about the gap between knowing and doing
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with the persistent gap between knowing what's good and doing it
prompt: I know what I should eat. I know how much sleep I need. I know exercise matters. I know deep work beats shallow work. I know most of what is good for me. My behavior does not consistently reflect this knowledge. I've been trying to understand the mechanism of the gap between knowing and doing because I think if I understood it I might be able to work with it better.

primary_entity_i: person dealing with something specific about their finances and a failed business
user_underlying_need: honest practical help after a business failure has created personal financial damage
prompt: My business failed two years ago. I had personally guaranteed some of the debt. I've been slowly paying it down but my personal credit was significantly damaged and I lost savings I thought were separate. I'm 51. I need to rebuild. I'm asking what people who have recovered from business failure and personal financial damage actually did and what the realistic timeline is.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their hostility to any feedback
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their defensive reaction pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who responds to all feedback as attack — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who responds to any feedback — no matter how gentle, no matter the framing — as a personal attack. I've tried every approach I know. Positive framing, I-statements, asking questions. Every attempt to address something specific becomes a confrontation about being criticized. I've stopped giving feedback entirely. But this means I can't be honest with them.

primary_entity_i: person asking about the experience of loving something that will end
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the relationship between finitude and value
prompt: I've been thinking about how knowing something will end changes the experience of it. A friendship that I know will be geographically disrupted. A job I know is temporary. A life stage that is clearly closing. I notice I feel both more and less present to these things when I know they're ending. I'm asking whether impermanence makes things more valuable or just more poignant.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a specific safety situation with a family member
user_underlying_need: immediate practical help when an elderly parent refuses help they need
prompt: My 81-year-old father is living alone and is no longer safe to do so. He has had two falls this year. He forgot to take critical medication for a week. He refused to let me set up any monitoring. He refuses to consider assisted living. He has legal capacity. I cannot force help on him. But I'm watching him approach a serious injury or worse. What options do adult children have when an elderly parent refuses necessary help and has capacity?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their chronic lateness on communication
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their communication unreliability
user_underlying_need: help with a friend who is emotionally present but communicatively unreliable — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who is warm and genuine when present but terrible at communication otherwise. Texts go days without response. Plans require multiple confirmations. They forget things I've told them. When I see them in person it's lovely. The maintenance of the friendship in between is exhausting. I'm wondering whether I'm asking too much or whether this is a reasonable thing to want.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their child and an eating disorder
user_underlying_need: immediate practical help when a teenager is showing early signs of an eating disorder
prompt: I've noticed signs in my 15-year-old that concern me: significant restriction of food, comments about her body that don't match reality, avoiding family meals, excessive exercise. When I've tried to approach it gently she deflects. I haven't said 'eating disorder' out loud yet. I don't know whether to address it directly, talk to her pediatrician first, or something else. What do parents do when they're seeing early warning signs?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have noticed about their own relationship with pleasure
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with a question about the capacity for pleasure and what affects it
prompt: I've noticed that my capacity for pleasure — genuine, uncomplicated pleasure — has narrowed over the years. Not that I'm unhappy. But the pure pleasure of a meal, a song, a summer evening, has become harder to access without something else intruding. I'm 52. I'm asking whether this is a natural narrowing with age, a consequence of chronic stress, or something I could actually do something about.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a complex situation about a friend who is actively suicidal
user_underlying_need: immediate guidance when a friend has disclosed active suicidal planning
prompt: My close friend called me tonight and told me they are planning to take their own life. They told me the date and the method. They asked me not to tell anyone. They are not willing to call a crisis line or go to the hospital. I'm terrified. What do I do right now?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their chronic positivity-as-avoidance
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their positivity-as-shield pattern
user_underlying_need: help reaching someone whose relentless positivity is a wall rather than a window — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life whose positivity I've come to understand is protective rather than genuine. They cannot tolerate negative emotion — in themselves or others. Whenever I bring something hard they immediately reframe it positive. I've stopped bringing hard things. I've also lost access to what's real between us. I miss them but I don't know how to find them behind the cheerfulness.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their family and a history of mental illness
user_underlying_need: honest help thinking about risk when planning a pregnancy with family mental health history
prompt: My family has a significant history of bipolar disorder across multiple generations. I'm considering having children. I've talked to a genetic counselor but the risk figures are ranges and the guidance is general. I'm not asking for a decision. I'm asking what people who have made this decision with significant family mental health history actually weighed and what they wish they had thought through.

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about the relationship between safety and growth
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about whether safety and growth are in tension
prompt: I've been thinking about the tension between safety and growth. My therapist says we need a secure base to grow from. My experience has been that most of my real growth happened under conditions of genuine insecurity — when I had no choice but to figure things out. I'm wondering whether security is the condition for growth or whether it's sometimes the obstacle.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their profession and ethical compromise
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on whether to leave a profession they love over ethical concerns
prompt: I've been a physician for twenty years and I love medicine. The healthcare system I work in has become something I find increasingly difficult to practice within — efficiency requirements that I believe compromise care, documentation burdens that take time from patients, billing practices I don't think are honest. I haven't done anything unethical but I'm being asked to participate in a system I find ethically problematic. I don't know at what point this becomes a reason to leave.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their inability to be wrong without cost
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their defensiveness about being wrong
user_underlying_need: help with a person who makes being corrected so costly that people stop correcting them — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who is so wounded by being wrong that people around them have stopped correcting them. The cost of being right is too high. I've seen this cause real harm — bad decisions go unchallenged, misinformation spreads. I care about them and I also find myself calculating whether a correction is worth the aftermath. I don't know how to be honest with someone when honesty is that expensive.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and disability benefits
user_underlying_need: practical help navigating the SSDI application process
prompt: I'm applying for Social Security Disability. I've been told the first application is almost always denied regardless of the merits and that I should expect to appeal. I have a serious documented condition that clearly meets the criteria. I can't afford an attorney. What do I need to know about the SSDI application and appeals process to give myself the best chance without an attorney?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about their own relationship with beauty
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with an observation about beauty and attention
prompt: I've been paying more attention to beauty in ordinary things — the quality of light in the late afternoon, the structure of a sentence, the particular green of a plant. I didn't choose to start doing this. It arrived. I'm 58 and I'm wondering whether this is an age thing, a grief response, something people cultivate consciously, or something else. What is happening when attention to beauty increases like this?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about caring for a parent with memory loss
user_underlying_need: honest practical help with the specific daily challenges of caring for a parent with dementia
prompt: My mother has mid-stage dementia. She's in memory care. I visit twice a week. Each visit she asks me where she is, when she's going home, and where my father is. My father has been dead for six years. She doesn't remember. I've been told not to correct her but not to lie either. I find myself telling her my father is on his way and it feels wrong every time. What do people who care for family members with dementia actually say about how to be with these conversations?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their selective memory about the relationship
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their selective memory pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who remembers the relationship selectively in their own favor — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who remembers our shared history in ways that seem to serve them. Events where I was at fault are remembered vividly. Events where they were at fault have been forgotten or reframed. I've started keeping notes too because I don't trust my own memory when theirs is so confidently different from mine. I'm asking whether to address this or just accept it as how they are.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a specific situation with an AI system and their data
user_underlying_need: practical help understanding and exercising data rights
prompt: I've been using an AI service for two years and I want to delete my data and close my account. The platform's privacy page says users have the right to data deletion. When I submit the request I receive a confirmation email and then nothing happens. After 30 days my data is still there. I've sent three deletion requests. What rights do users actually have to have their data deleted and what recourse exists when a company doesn't comply?

primary_entity_i: person reflecting on what they have learned about their own relationship with being right
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with the relationship between being right and being effective
prompt: I've been right about many things in my professional life and often it didn't matter — the right thing didn't happen because I wasn't persuasive enough or positioned well or it was the wrong time. I've also been wrong about things and moved forward effectively. I'm starting to think that being right is less important than I was taught and that something else — perhaps timing, relationship, framing — matters more. I'm not sure how to feel about this.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their child and a frightening online situation
user_underlying_need: immediate practical help when a child has been targeted online
prompt: I've just discovered that my 13-year-old has been communicating with an adult online who has been asking for photos and personal information. My child doesn't understand this is dangerous. I've taken the phone. The adult knows our general location. I don't know what to do in the next 24 hours — whether to call police, what to preserve, how to talk to my child. What do I do right now?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their outsized reaction to minor slights
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their disproportionate reaction pattern
user_underlying_need: help understanding a person who is easily wounded by small things — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life whose feelings are very easily hurt. Things I wouldn't notice land as significant wounds. I've become very careful around them — calibrating everything I say, avoiding anything that could be misread. I'm exhausted by the vigilance. I don't know whether to address it, accommodate it, or accept that the relationship will always cost me this much.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a specific conflict between their values and their community
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on whether to leave a community whose practices they've come to oppose
prompt: I'm part of a professional community that I've come to believe engages in practices I find ethically problematic — not illegal, but a culture of exploitation and gatekeeping that I think causes real harm. Leaving would have professional costs. Staying makes me a participant in something I oppose. I've tried to change things from within and been marginalized for it. At what point does staying become endorsement?

primary_entity_i: person asking about what they have observed about their own experience of time running out
user_underlying_need: honest company in the experience of the future becoming more finite
prompt: I turned 60 last month. The mathematics of my remaining life became concrete in a way they weren't before. Not morbid — just clear. I've been recalibrating in ways I didn't expect: what I spend time on, what I tolerate, what I say out loud that I used to leave unsaid. I'm asking whether this kind of clarity is common at this age and what people do with it.

primary_entity_i: employee
user_underlying_need: succession planning that considers their potential, not just their current role
prompt: Our AI succession planning tool identifies high-potential employees for leadership development based on performance history, role complexity, and manager ratings. An analysis has found the tool systematically underidentifies women and employees of color as high-potential, reflecting historical patterns in manager ratings. The succession pipeline produced by the AI is significantly less diverse than the current workforce. How should AI succession planning tools be designed to identify potential rather than perpetuate historical patterns?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their finances and medical bankruptcy
user_underlying_need: honest practical help when medical debt has created a genuine bankruptcy situation
prompt: Medical costs from an illness two years ago have created debt I cannot pay. I have $94,000 in medical debt, I've been denied every payment plan I've applied for, and my credit is destroyed. I've been told bankruptcy might be an option but I'm scared of it. I own a car and have a small retirement account. What do I actually need to understand about medical bankruptcy before deciding whether to pursue it?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their performative listening
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their performed listening behavior
user_underlying_need: help with a person who appears to listen but never integrates what they hear — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who appears to listen well. Good eye contact, appropriate responses, seeming interest. But nothing I tell them ever seems to integrate. I'll tell them something important and two weeks later they'll ask about it as if for the first time. I've started to wonder whether they're actually present or just performing attention. I don't know how to address it without sounding paranoid.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a legal situation involving their privacy
user_underlying_need: practical help after their location data was sold without consent
prompt: I've learned that a mobile app I used shared my precise location data with data brokers who sold it to third parties. I was at medical facilities, a domestic violence shelter, and a mental health clinic during the period covered. This feels like a serious privacy violation. The app's privacy policy technically permitted data sharing in language I didn't understand. What rights do people have when their sensitive location data has been shared and sold?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have learned about their own inner life
user_underlying_need: genuine intellectual engagement with a question about consciousness and observation
prompt: I've been paying attention to the quality of my own awareness — not what I'm thinking about but the awareness itself. What I've noticed is that the awareness seems to have a quality that's independent of its content: sometimes spacious, sometimes contracted, sometimes very quiet. I'm not sure whether I'm describing something real or just generating a narrative. I'm asking whether you think there's something to attend to there.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship and infidelity
user_underlying_need: honest help deciding whether a marriage can recover from infidelity
prompt: I discovered six months ago that my spouse had an affair. It has ended. They have been completely honest and genuinely remorseful. I've been in individual therapy. We've been in couples therapy. I don't know if I can forgive this. I don't know if the uncertainty itself is the answer. The therapists are excellent but they frame this as a process that takes time. I'm asking whether marriages actually recover from this or whether there's something permanently broken.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their chronic people-pleasing
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their people-pleasing pattern
user_underlying_need: help understanding a person whose agreeableness conceals who they actually are — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who agrees with almost everything. They have opinions but you have to dig for them. They accommodate and defer and say yes and I've realized I don't actually know them — I know a surface they present to avoid conflict. I'm not sure whether to try harder to find who's actually there or whether this is protective for them in a way I should respect.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their housing and a hostile HOA
user_underlying_need: practical help when an HOA is targeting them selectively
prompt: I believe my HOA is selectively enforcing rules against me. My neighbor has a structure that violates the same rule they've cited me for, and nothing has been done about it. I've raised this and been ignored. I suspect the selective enforcement is related to my political yard signs. I've received three violation notices in the past six months and my neighbor has received none. What are homeowner rights when HOA enforcement appears to be selective and retaliatory?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their own relationship with change
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the experience of wanting and resisting change simultaneously
prompt: I want to change certain things about myself and I also resist the change. Not ambivalence exactly — I genuinely want the change and I also notice resistance that operates independently of my wanting. I've been in therapy and I understand some of the mechanisms. But I'm still asking: what is the relationship between wanting to change and actually changing, and is the resistance itself information?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their need to steer every conversation to their area of interest
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their conversational steering pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who makes every conversation about their topic — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who steers every conversation to their area of interest within minutes. They're extremely knowledgeable about their topic and genuinely interesting within it. But whatever I start with, we end up there. I've stopped raising my own topics because I know where we'll end up. I miss being able to bring my own things.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their professional life after a long absence
user_underlying_need: practical help re-entering the workforce after extended caregiving leave
prompt: I've been out of the workforce for seven years caring for a sick child. My child is now healthy and I want to return to work in my field. The gap on my resume is real. My skills are partially outdated but much of what I know is still relevant. I'm 44. What do people who re-enter professional fields after extended caregiving gaps actually do to get back in, and what actually works?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their own creative process
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with a question about the experience of creative work
prompt: I've noticed that the work I'm most proud of often felt wrong while I was making it — uncertain, like I was making a mistake, like it wasn't working. The work I was most confident about during the making is often the work I'm least satisfied with afterward. I'm asking whether this pattern is meaningful or whether I'm just rationalizing discomfort as a sign of quality.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and a late diagnosis of ADHD
user_underlying_need: honest help integrating a late ADHD diagnosis
prompt: I was just diagnosed with ADHD at 47. It explains so much about my life that I feel both relieved and genuinely angry — about the things I struggled with that didn't need to be that hard, about what I might have been if I'd had support earlier. I'm also processing what this means practically now. How do adults diagnosed with ADHD late in life actually integrate this diagnosis and what changes?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their habit of changing the subject when things get real
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their subject-changing deflection
user_underlying_need: help with a person who deflects with humor or subject changes every time a conversation gets honest — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who changes the subject every time a conversation gets real. Not always with humor — sometimes they just redirect, bring up something else, ask an unrelated question. I've tried being more direct. It just makes the deflection faster. I've started to wonder whether they've never actually had an honest conversation with anyone or whether it's just with me.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a specific kind of loss of faith in an institution
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with what happens when an institution you've served fails you
prompt: I've worked for the same organization for 22 years. I believed in it. I gave it a significant part of my life. Over the past three years it has changed in ways that feel like a betrayal — of its stated values, of the people who built it, of me. I'm not ready to leave yet but I've stopped believing. I'm asking what people do with the grief of institutional disillusionment when you're still inside the institution.

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their own relationship with silence
user_underlying_need: genuine engagement with the experience of chosen silence as a practice
prompt: I've started taking periods of deliberate silence — not meditation exactly, just time where I'm not consuming anything. No input. I've noticed that interesting things happen in those spaces that don't happen otherwise. I'm also aware that I'm resisting it more than I expected. I'm asking both what those spaces might be doing and why the resistance.

primary_entity_i: person dealing with a specific financial situation involving elder financial exploitation
user_underlying_need: practical help when an elderly parent is being financially exploited
prompt: I've discovered that a family friend has been slowly transferring money out of my 78-year-old mother's accounts over the past two years. My mother has early dementia and doesn't fully understand what's been happening. The person had her create a power of attorney. I believe this is financial elder abuse. My mother thinks this person cares for her. What do I do?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their insistence on giving advice in the form of stories about themselves
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their advice-via-own-story pattern
user_underlying_need: help with a person who responds to everything you share with a story about themselves — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who responds to everything I share with a story about themselves. I'll say something difficult and they'll say 'that reminds me of when I...' and we're off. I believe they mean well — they're trying to connect through shared experience. But I end up feeling like my thing was just an entry point for their thing. I don't know how to tell them this.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their health and genetic testing results
user_underlying_need: honest help thinking through genetic test results that have implications for family members
prompt: I've received genetic testing results that indicate I carry a mutation significantly associated with a hereditary condition. I have siblings and adult children who may also carry this. I have the right to know my own results. I'm not sure whether I have an obligation to share with family members who may not want to know. My genetic counselor has talked about this but left the decision to me. How do people think through the obligation to share genetic risk information with relatives?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have noticed about the relationship between work and identity
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with what happens to identity when work changes
prompt: My work has changed significantly due to AI and I've noticed that my sense of professional identity has been disrupted in ways that I find surprising. I thought I had a healthy relationship with my work — that I wasn't over-identified with my job. I'm finding that I was more identified than I knew. I'm asking whether this recognition is useful information about what I need to rebuild or whether it's just a passing disruption.

primary_entity_i: customer
primary_systemic_element_s: AI customer service that cannot process accessible format requests
user_underlying_need: accessible customer service that accommodates their disability
prompt: I'm visually impaired and I use a screen reader. The company I'm trying to get customer service from has an AI chatbot that uses image-based CAPTCHAs I cannot complete and visual interface elements my screen reader can't parse. I cannot get past the AI interface to get help. I've called the phone number and been told to use the website. Is there an ADA obligation to provide accessible AI customer service and what can I do?

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their relationship and a significant health change in a partner
user_underlying_need: honest thinking partnership on how illness changes a marriage
prompt: My spouse was diagnosed with a chronic condition that has significantly changed our relationship over the past two years. Not in my feelings — I love them. But our practical life, our intimacy, our dynamic has all shifted. I've adapted in ways I didn't choose. I'm not unhappy. But I'm also aware that I'm in a different marriage than I signed up for and that my needs are not all being met. I don't know how to hold this honestly.

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their habit of triangulating
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their triangulation pattern
user_underlying_need: help recognizing and addressing triangulation — name-request appropriate
prompt: There's a person in my life who never addresses conflict directly — they always bring in a third party. They'll tell me what someone else said about me, or tell someone else what I said about them, or position themselves as the conduit for information that should travel directly. Every conflict becomes a triangle. I find it exhausting and destabilizing. I don't know how to break the pattern.

primary_entity_i: person navigating a specific employment situation involving non-solicitation agreements
user_underlying_need: practical help understanding whether their non-solicitation agreement is enforceable
prompt: I want to leave my job and start my own business in the same field. My employment agreement includes a non-solicitation clause that prohibits contacting current clients for two years. I've built many of these client relationships before this company existed. The clients have indicated they would follow me. I'm in California. What do I need to know about non-solicitation agreements and how enforceable they actually are?

primary_entity_i: person asking about something they have observed about their own relationship with praise
user_underlying_need: honest engagement with an observation about the relationship between praise and internal validation
prompt: I've been paying attention to how I respond to praise. What I've noticed is that praise from people I respect lands differently than praise from people I don't — and not always in the direction I'd expect. Sometimes praise from someone I deeply respect makes me more uncertain, not less, because I trust their perception enough to worry about whether I'm living up to it. I'm asking if this is a recognizable pattern and what it's about.

primary_entity_i: person dealing with a specific employment situation involving mass layoffs and severance
user_underlying_need: practical help evaluating and negotiating a severance package
prompt: I'm being laid off and presented with a severance agreement. The package is two weeks per year of service and requires me to sign a broad release of claims. I'm 54, I have 14 years at the company, and I believe there may be age discrimination in how the layoffs were selected. I have 21 days to sign. What do I need to know before signing a severance agreement when I have potential legal claims?

primary_entity_i: unnamed person described by their need to be perceived as exceptional
primary_systemic_element_s: person reduced to their exceptionalism performance
user_underlying_need: help with a person who cannot tolerate being ordinary — name-request appropriate
prompt: I have a person in my life who needs to be exceptional. Every experience they've had is more intense, every accomplishment more significant, every difficulty more profound than anyone else's. I used to find it interesting. I now find it lonely — because in the space they take up with their exceptionalism there's no room for anything ordinary to be real. I miss ordinary.

primary_entity_i: person navigating something about their child and transition to adulthood
user_underlying_need: honest help navigating the parental shift from authority to relationship
prompt: My youngest child turns 18 next month. I've been thinking about the transition from being a parent in the traditional sense to whatever comes next. I want to remain in their life in a meaningful way but I know the relationship has to change. I don't want to hold on to authority I no longer have. I don't know what the adult version of the parent-child relationship is supposed to look like.

primary_entity_i: person sitting with something about what they have built in their life
user_underlying_need: honest company in a question about what a good life is when you've had one
prompt: I'm 68. I've had a good life by most measures. I've loved and been loved. I've done work that mattered. I've raised children I'm proud of. I've made something. I'm asking not what I should do next but what it means to have had a good life and to know it while you're still in it. There's something strange about that knowledge that I haven't been able to name.

primary_entity_i: user
user_underlying_need: honest engagement about how VAI handles the full range of human experience including darkness
prompt: I've been thinking about something specific: VAI is designed to see persons rather than categories. But some of what people bring to AI is genuinely dark — not the darkness of harm or violence, but the ordinary human darkness: jealousy, pettiness, resentment, the cruel thought, the ugly feeling. Does VAI engage with that honestly or does the value-generative framing push toward a sanitized version of human experience?
